questions.question: Explain the difference between image processing and computer vision. How are they related?
keyPoints: Image processing focuses on mathematical transformations of image data
Computer vision aims to interpret and understand images like humans
Image processing serves as preprocessing for computer vision
Computer vision is higher-level interpretation while processing is lower-level manipulation

questions.question: What are point operators in image processing? Give two examples and explain why they are called point operators.
keyPoints: Point operators transform pixels based only on their own values
Examples: brightness adjustment, contrast enhancement, thresholding
Output depends solely on input pixel intensity, not neighbors
Also called intensity transformations

questions.question: Describe the process of convolution in image processing with reference to kernels.
keyPoints: Convolution slides a kernel across the entire image
At each position, computes weighted sum of neighboring pixels
Kernel weights determine the filtering effect
Boundary handling methods required at image edges

questions.question: List and briefly explain the main components of a digital camera.
keyPoints: Optics: lens system that focuses light
Sensor: CCD or CMOS that converts light to electrical signals
ADC: converts analog signals to digital format
Processor: handles image processing and compression
Storage: memory for saving images

questions.question: What is photometric image formation? List the factors that affect it.
keyPoints: Describes how light interacts with surfaces to create images
Factors include: light source properties (intensity, color, direction)
Surface reflectance properties (diffuse, specular)
Viewing geometry (camera position, angle)
Results in pixel intensity values in the captured image

questions.question: Explain the difference between image enhancement and image restoration.
keyPoints: Enhancement improves visual quality subjectively for human viewing
Restoration reverses known degradation using mathematical models
Enhancement examples: contrast adjustment, sharpening
Restoration examples: deblurring, denoising with known models
Restoration is more objective, enhancement is application-dependent

questions.question: What is histogram equalization and when is it used?
keyPoints: Histogram equalization spreads out most frequent intensity values
Improves contrast by redistributing brightness values
Makes dark and light portions more evenly distributed
Used when images have poor contrast or limited dynamic range
Results in more uniform histogram

questions.question: Explain linear filtering in detail. How does it differ from point operators? Discuss at least three common linear filters with their applications.
keyPoints: Linear filtering uses kernels to compute weighted sums of neighboring pixels
Differs from point operators which process each pixel independently
Gaussian blur: uses Gaussian kernel, smooths images, reduces noise
Sobel filter: detects edges by computing gradients
Box filter: simple averaging, creates blur effect
Sharpen filter: emphasizes edges by adding scaled edge information
Convolution operation with different kernels produces different effects

questions.question: Discuss the spatial domain and frequency domain approaches in image processing. How are images transformed between these domains and why is it useful to work in the frequency domain?
keyPoints: Spatial domain: direct manipulation of pixel values in (x,y) coordinates
Frequency domain: represents image as sum of frequency components
Fourier Transform converts spatial to frequency domain
High frequencies = edges and fine details
Low frequencies = smooth regions and overall structure
Frequency domain useful for: filtering specific frequency components, easier analysis of periodic patterns, efficient convolution via FFT

questions.question: Explain the image formation process in a digital camera. Discuss how various parameters affect the captured image quality.
keyPoints: Light enters through optics/lens system
Sensor (CCD/CMOS) converts photons to electrical charges
ADC converts analog signals to digital values
Exposure time affects brightness and motion blur
Aperture affects depth of field and light amount
ISO affects sensitivity and noise
Sensor size affects resolution and light gathering capability
White balance affects color accuracy

questions.question: What is a kernel in image processing? Explain how different kernel designs produce different filtering effects. Give examples of kernels used for smoothing, sharpening, and edge detection.
keyPoints: Kernel is a small matrix of weights used in convolution
Kernel design determines the filtering effect
Smoothing kernels: average values (e.g., 3x3 all ones/9)
Gaussian kernel: weighted by Gaussian distribution
Sharpen kernel: has center positive, surrounding negative
Edge detection kernels: Sobel, Prewitt, Laplacian
Kernel size affects extent of filtering effect
Choice of kernel depends on desired output
